era_context: Late Bronze Age, mid-18th Dynasty Egypt under Thutmose III. The New Kingdom is consolidating its territorial gains in the Levant and Nubia. The Mitanni kingdom is a major power in Upper Mesopotamia. The Mycenaean civilization is developing in Greece. This is an era of intensive international trade, military consolidation, and monumental construction.

events.title: Thutmose III's Levantine campaigns and tribute collection
events.description: Thutmose III continued his systematic military campaigns in the Levant as part of his program of territorial consolidation and tribute extraction. These campaigns were conducted regularly (often annually or biennially) and are extensively documented in the Karnak temple inscriptions. The campaigns reinforced Egyptian hegemony and ensured the flow of luxury goods to Egypt. This period represents the consolidation phase of New Kingdom expansion.

sources.name: Karnak Temple inscriptions (Annals of Thutmose III)

events.certainty_note: Thutmose III's campaigns are extensively documented in contemporary inscriptions and confirmed by archaeological evidence.

disconfirming_evidence: The specific dating of individual Thutmose III campaigns is uncertain. The Karnak inscriptions do not provide year-by-year dating; precise assignment to specific years is often speculative. The 'Egyptian chronology problem' affects all dating in this period by ±10–20 years. Additionally, the extent of Egyptian control over the Levant may have been more episodic and fragile than monumental inscriptions suggest.
historiographic_note: The record for 1462 BCE is heavily dependent on Egyptian temple inscriptions, which are propagandistic. The perspective is Egyptocentric; the viewpoints of Levantine and other Near Eastern polities are largely invisible. Chronological uncertainty is the dominant problem affecting all dating in this period.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of the military consolidation initiated by the Battle of Megiddo
